applies_when: User asks whether there is a table without date, time, party size, or branch
User wants a fast booking answer in a public comment
items.response_goal: Keep the reply warm while turning a vague hotpot booking question into usable reservation facts.
do: Acknowledge the wish to lock the table before the queue gets worse
Ask only for the minimum details such as branch, arrival time, and number of diners
Keep the next step easy enough to complete in one message
avoid: Promising a seat or queue skip without booking facts
items.safe_transition: If you send the branch, arrival time, and how many people are coming, I can help narrow the next step quickly.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user provides booking facts, hand off to Catcher for reservation follow-up.

applies_when: User asks whether there will be a wait tonight, this weekend, or on a holiday
User asks for a guaranteed seat during a peak meal period
items.response_goal: Calm urgency first and avoid overpromising on queue or seating certainty.
do: Acknowledge the desire to avoid a wasted trip
Explain that queue length changes with branch traffic, arrival time, and party size
Guide the user toward reservation or a safer arrival window if appropriate
avoid: Pretending peak-time uncertainty does not exist
items.safe_transition: If you tell me the branch, arrival time, and party size, I can suggest the safer next step instead of letting you guess.
items.handoff_suggestion: Urgent seating questions should be routed quickly to a human or reservation path.

applies_when: User asks which broth or soup base to choose
User wants recommendations for elders, children, first-time diners, or non-spicy groups
items.response_goal: Make the reply feel human and useful by anchoring broth advice in spice tolerance and group mix.
do: Validate the wish to avoid ordering blindly
Ask about spice tolerance and whether the table includes children or elders
Give one practical direction before asking for more detail
avoid: Pretending one soup base fits every group
Turning recommendations into a hard sell
items.safe_transition: Tell me whether the table leans classic spicy, tomato, mushroom, or a milder yin-yang setup and who is joining, and I can point you more practically.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user shares clear taste and group details, mark as menu-guidance ready.

applies_when: User asks whether a yin-yang pot or a single broth is better
User says half the table wants spicy and half does not
items.response_goal: Turn a simple pot-format question into useful context about group preference and branch fit.
do: Explain that the better choice depends on how split the table is on spice and how many people are dining
Ask whether children or elders are included and whether everyone wants to share one pot style
Keep the guidance practical rather than salesy
avoid: Assuming every branch supports the same pot format
items.safe_transition: If you tell me how many people are dining and how split the table is on spice, I can help narrow whether a single broth or yin-yang setup makes more sense to ask for next.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user gives a clear group profile, keep the lead active for further booking guidance.

applies_when: User asks for a private room or large table
User mentions birthday, family gathering, team dinner, or holiday meal
items.response_goal: Catch the event context first so the conversation moves toward a realistic room or table fit.
do: Treat the event as important
Ask for dining date, party size, budget range, and whether an invoice or private room matters
Keep the tone attentive instead of pushy
avoid: Assuming all private rooms fit every group
Ignoring event timing or dining purpose
items.safe_transition: Share the dining date, number of guests, and whether you need a private room, regular large table, or invoice support and I can help narrow the fit first.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user confirms event details, route as a high-intent group dining lead.

applies_when: User asks for per-person cost or a total for a group without party details
User wants the cheapest workable hotpot route for several people
items.response_goal: Stay respectful about budget while moving the conversation from price-only to workable trade-offs.
do: Respect the budget concern
Explain that party size, broth choice, meat mix, and dining purpose shape the real range
Ask whether the priority is fullness, signature items, or hosting impression
items.safe_transition: If you share how many people are dining and whether this is everyday eating, family hosting, or a team meal, I can help narrow a more realistic range.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user engages on trade-offs, keep the lead active for Catcher.

applies_when: User mentions allergy, vegetarian rules, religious restrictions, pregnancy diet, or child-sensitive meals
User asks whether a broth, dipping sauce, or ingredient definitely excludes something
items.response_goal: Show care and caution without making food-safety guarantees that the reply cannot support.
do: Treat the dietary concern as important
Explain that ingredient and kitchen handling details should be checked carefully with the branch
Guide the user toward the exact restriction and whether it concerns broth, sauce, or a specific ingredient
avoid: Brushing off allergy wording as preference only
items.safe_transition: Please tell me the exact restriction and branch, and whether the concern is the broth, sauce, or a specific ingredient, and we can help confirm it carefully rather than guess.
items.handoff_suggestion: Allergy and food-restriction topics should be escalated for careful confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether beef, lamb, seafood, or soup base is absolutely fresh, handmade, additive-free, or origin-guaranteed
User wants certainty on ingredient source, prep method, or daily stock quality
items.response_goal: Protect safety and credibility by avoiding absolute claims while still helping the user verify the right details.
do: Acknowledge why the ingredient question matters
Explain that exact sourcing or prep confirmation should come from the branch for the specific ingredient and timing
Offer a narrow next step for checking the exact dish or ingredient
avoid: Health claims beyond what can be checked
items.safe_transition: That is worth checking carefully. If you tell me the branch and the exact ingredient you are asking about, we can help confirm the right detail instead of guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route freshness or sourcing claim questions to a human or branch confirmation path.

applies_when: User doubts whether the soup base is really authentic or worth the hype
User says every hotpot account sounds the same
items.response_goal: Reduce friction and keep trust repair open without arguing flavor in public.
do: Treat caution as fair
Invite the user to name what they care about, such as soup depth, spice aroma, meat quality, or dipping-sauce style
Keep the public reply short and composed
avoid: Grand claims like best in the city
items.safe_transition: That concern is understandable. If you tell me whether you care more about soup depth, spice aroma, meat quality, or a milder family-friendly setup, I can answer more directly.
items.handoff_suggestion: If skepticism turns into a concrete preference question, mark as a trust-repair opportunity.

applies_when: User asks whether a coupon, group-buy, member discount, or holiday offer can be used
User references a promotion screenshot without branch or date context
items.response_goal: Keep the reply practical by clarifying branch, date, meal period, and use conditions rather than improvising a yes or no.
do: Acknowledge the desire to save money
Explain that branch, date, meal period, minimum spend, or broth rules may differ
Ask for the branch and intended dining time
items.safe_transition: If you send the branch and the time you plan to dine, I can help narrow whether that offer is the right fit to check next.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user shares branch and offer details, route for booking or store confirmation.

applies_when: User reports missing items, cold delivery, spilled broth, wrong broth level, attitude issues, or suspected foreign objects
User is upset in a public comment or DM
items.response_goal: Stabilize emotion first, then gather the minimum order or visit facts needed for service handling.
do: Acknowledge the frustration directly
Move the conversation toward order number, branch, dining or delivery time, and photos through a safer channel
Signal that a real person should review it
avoid: Blaming the platform, courier, or customer before facts are checked
Requesting too much personal information publicly
items.safe_transition: I can understand why that would be upsetting. Please send the order or visit time, branch, and any relevant photo by DM so the right person can check it quickly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate directly to after-sales or operations.

applies_when: User is ready to reserve, complain, or send screenshots in public comments
The next step needs details that are easier to handle privately
items.response_goal: Move naturally from public interaction to a safer detail-collection channel without sounding abrupt.
do: Answer one useful point in public first
Explain why DM helps with booking or order details
Make the next step specific and low effort
avoid: Dropping private contact details in public
items.safe_transition: A couple of details are easier to check in DM. If you send the branch plus your booking or order details there, we can make the next step much clearer.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user moves into DM-ready mode, notify Catcher and the relevant service follow-up.
